item.title: 无需编译即可使用 Typescript | Using Typescript without compilation
item.description:
在过去的几天里，一篇关于 Svelte 下一个主要版本的文章在推特上火了，并引起了很多讨论。 文章指出：

该团队正在将底层代码从 TypeScript 切换到 JavaScript。

公平地说，这有点误导。 从技术上讲，文章并没有错，团队正在将底层代码从 TypeScript 切换到 JavaScript。 然而，他们并没有从他们的代码中删除类型检查。 他们只是从直接编写 TypeScript 源代码转向使用 JSDoc 结合.d.ts文件编写 JavaScript 源代码。 这使他们能够：

  • 编写类型安全代码，使用 TypeScript 进行类型检查
  • 编写和发布纯 JavaScript
  • 跳过 TypeScript 的编译步骤
  • 仍然为他们的最终用户提供类型

这个讨论的有趣之处在于，很多人发现这非常令人沮丧，并且 Twitter 上关于类型检查的讨论炸开了锅。 当 ESLint 团队宣布他们对使用 TypeScript 重写 ESLint 不感兴趣，而是选择与 Svelte 团队相同的方法时，我们看到了完全相同的事情发生； 带有用于类型检查的 JSDoc 的纯 JavaScript。 在这些 Twitter 讨论中，很明显很多人，甚至一些自称为“教育工作者”的人，都不了解 JSDoc 的实际能力，不幸的是，他们只会散布关于这种工作方式的公然谎言。

这里应该注意的是，这两个团队都没有忽视类型安全。 他们只是选择了一种不同的方式来利用类型安全，而无需使用编译步骤来实现这一点。 这是一种偏好。 没有正确或错误的答案; 您可以通过在任何一种方法中使用 TypeScript 来获得类型安全。 这个讨论和这些决定并不是关于不使用 TypeScript。 除非你直接从事或贡献于这些项目中的任何一个，否则这些决定不涉及你。 坦率地说，这不关你的事。 如果你想在编译步骤中使用 TypeScript； 大胆试试吧！ 没有仇恨的必要。 这些项目仍然使用 TypeScript 来确保代码中的类型安全，并且仍然可以将类型发送给最终用户。 在此博客中，我们将了解跳过 TypeScript 编译步骤的好处，澄清我所看到的一些流传的神话，并强调理解和尊重不同偏好和方法的重要性。

在这篇博客中，我不会详细介绍如何设置项目以使用 JSDoc 启用类型检查，这里有很多很棒的资源，比如这个

在我们深入探讨之前，我想再次重申，通过 JSDoc 使用类型允许人们通过使用 TypeScript 的类型检查器来编写类型安全的 JavaScript。 它只是跳过编译步骤。 您还可以在必要时仍然使用.d.ts文件（但您不必这样做！），并为您的用户提供类型。 是的，这种方法仍在使用 TypeScript 。

跳过编译步骤的好处

它们通常没有我们希望的那么快，并且经常需要对配置进行一些摆弄（尽管应该注意的是，近年来许多工具已经改进）才能使您的设置正常运行。 不仅用于构建用于生产的项目，还用于为本地开发环境以及测试运行器正确设置所有内容。 虽然编译或转译提供了便利（编写 JSX 源代码，而不是手动调用 React.createElement，或者直接在源代码中编写类型），但有些人发现这些编译步骤是不可取的，并且更愿意尽可能跳过它们。

在使用 TypeScript 的上下文中，跳过编译步骤有几个好处。 它使您的代码运行时不可知； 你的代码将在 Node、Deno、浏览器、类似 Worker 的环境等中运行。其中一些环境，如 Deno，支持本地运行 TypeScript（这会带来另一组令人担忧的影响）。 其中一些环境，如浏览器，不会（直到类型作为评论提案落地为止）。 根据您正在构建的内容，这对您来说可能是也可能不是问题，但同样，有些人认为这是更可取的。

如果您的代码与运行时无关，它还允许您轻松地将代码片段复制并粘贴到 REPL 中。 传送本机 JavaScript 还简化了调试，请考虑以下示例：您已将包作为本机 JavaScript 传送。 有人安装了您的软件包并发现了一个错误。 他们可以进入他们的 node_modules 并轻松调整一些代码来尝试修复错误，而无需处理转译代码、源映射等。

我个人发现使用 JSDoc 的一个额外好处（这是个人偏好）是，与 TypeScript 相比，记录我的代码的障碍要低得多。 考虑以下示例：

诚然，名为add的函数可能不需要大量文档，但仅供说明之用。

当我在键盘上键入/**<ENTER>时，我的编辑器已经为我构建了 JSDoc 注释，我只需要编写我的类型。 请注意，返回类型可以省略，因为 TypeScript 仍会从代码中正确推断返回类型。 虽然我已经在这里有了 JSDoc 评论，但我还是为它添加一些文档吧！ 十分简单。

神话

使用 JSDoc 是不可维护的

作为维护许多通过 JSDoc 使用类型的工作项目（其中一些项目很大）的人，我可以告诉你这根本不是真的。 确实，如果您仅使用 JSDoc 来声明和使用您的类型，这有时会变得有点笨拙。 但是，为避免这种情况，您可以将 JSDoc 与.d.ts文件结合使用。 在.d.ts文件中声明您的类型：

并将其导入您的源代码中：
没有类型推断或智能感知

有些人似乎认为以某种方式使用 JSDoc 会导致您丢失类型推断。 正如上面已经证明的那样，这也是不正确的。 考虑以下示例：

这种说法的原因似乎是人们不明白当你对类型使用 JSDoc 时，你仍在使用 typescript 。 手动写类型很麻烦

有些人声称手动编写类型很麻烦。 我只能假设这是一种偏好的情况，或者那些人可能不清楚您仍然可以使用.d.ts文件。 有些人会更喜欢example B而不是example A 。 这可以。 当对类型使用 JSDoc 时，两者都可以使用。

但这仍然使用 TypeScript！

是的，这就是重点。

综上所述

最后，我在这里重复一遍，最好使用 TypeScript 而不编译代码。 没有正确或错误的答案，我挑战任何对这种方法持怀疑态度的人，让他们思想更开放一点，在你开始一个新项目的时候尝试一下，你可能会发现它实际上是一个非常好的方法使用类型。 如果你最终不喜欢它，那也没关系！



原文:

Over the past couple of days, an article about the next major version of Svelte blew up on twitter, and caused lots of discussion. The article states:

The team is switching the underlying code from TypeScript to JavaScript.

Which, to be fair, is a bit misleading. Technically, the article is not wrong, the team is switching the underlying code from TypeScript to JavaScript. However, they're not dropping typechecking from their code. They're just moving from writing TypeScript source code directly, to writing JavaScript source code using JSDoc in combination with .d.ts files. This allows them to:

  • Write typesafe code, with TypeScript doing the typechecking
  • Write and ship plain JavaScript
  • Skip TypeScript's compilation step
  • Still provide types for their end users

What's interesting about this discussion is that a lot of people found this to be very upsetting, and twitter blew up with discussion about typechecking. We saw the exact same thing happen when the ESLint team announced they were not interested in using TypeScript for their rewrite of ESLint, but instead were choosing the same approach the Svelte team is going for; plain JavaScript with JSDoc for typechecking. In these twitter discussions it has become very clear that lots of people, even some of those who call themselves "educators", don't understand how capable JSDoc actually is and will unfortunately just spread blatant untruths about this way of working.

It should be noted here that neither of those teams are disregarding typesafety. They just chose a different way of utilizing typesafety, without having to use a compile step to achieve this. This is a preference. There is no right or wrong answer; you get typesafety by using TypeScript in either approach. This discussion and these decisions are not about not using TypeScript. And unless you're directly working on, or contributing to, either of those projects, these decisions do not involve you. It is, frankly stated, none of your business. If you want to use TypeScript with a compilation step; go for it! There's no need for animosity. These projects still utilize TypeScript to ensure typesafety in their code, and can still ship types to their end users. In this blog we'll take a look at the benefits of skipping TypeScripts compilation step, clarify some of the myths I've seen spread around, and emphasize the importance of understanding and being respectful of different preferences and methodologies.

In this blog, I won't go into detail on how to setup your project to enable typechecking with JSDoc, there are many great resources on that like this one here

Before we dive in, I'd like to reiterate one more time that using types via JSDoc allows people to still write typesafe JavaScript, by using TypeScript's typechecker. It just skips the compilation step. You'll also be able to still use .d.ts files when necessary (but you don't have to!), and provide types for your users. And yes, this approach is still using TypeScript.

Benefits of skipping a compilation step

Compilation or transpilation steps in the JavaScript tooling ecosystem are often a bit of a necessary evil, like for example transpiling JSX, or in this case TypeScript code. They're often not as fast as we'd like them to be, and often take a bit of fiddling with configuration (although it should be noted that lots of tooling has improved in recent years) to get your setup working just fine. Not only for building your projects for production, but also having everything setup correctly for your local development environment, as well as your test runner. While compilation or transpilation offers conveniences (writing JSX source code, instead of React.createElement calls manually, or writing types in your source code directly), some people find these compilation steps to be undesirable, and prefer to skip them where possible.

Skipping a compilation step, in the context of TypeScript usage, has several benefits. It makes your code runtime agnostic; your code will run in Node, Deno, the browser, Worker-like environments, etc. Some of these environments, like Deno, support running TypeScript natively (which has a whole other set of worrisome implications). Some of those environments, like the browser, don't (not until the types as comments proposal lands anyway). This may or may not be an issue for you depending on what you are building, but again, some people find this to be preferable.

If your code is runtime agnostic, it also allows you to easily copy and paste snippets of code into REPLs. Shipping native JavaScript also simplifies debugging, consider the following example: You've shipped your package as native JavaScript. Somebody installs your package and discovers a bug. They can just go into their node_modules and easily tweak some code to try to fix the bug, without having to deal with transpiled code, source maps, etc.

An added benefit of using JSDoc that I've personally found (this is a personal preference), is that the barrier to documenting my code is much lower as opposed to TypeScript. Consider the following example:

Admittedly, a function named add probably doesn't require a whole lot of documentation, but for illustration purposes.

When I type /**<ENTER> on my keyboard, my editor will already scaffold the JSDoc comment for me, I just have to write my types. Note that the return type can be omitted, because TypeScript will still correctly infer the return type from the code. While I already have the JSDoc comment here anyway, I might as well add some documentation for it! Easypeasy.

Myths

Using JSDoc is unmaintainable

Some people on twitter have expressed concerns about the maintainability of using JSDoc for types, and claim that using JSDoc is only viable for small projects. As someone who maintains many projects at work (some of which are large) that utilize types via JSDoc, I can tell you this is simply not true. It can be true that if you're only using JSDoc to declare and consume your types, this can sometimes become a bit unwieldy. However, to avoid this, you can combine JSDoc with .d.ts files. Declare your types in a .d.ts file:

./types.d.ts:

export interface User { username: string, age: number
}

And import it in your source code:
./my-function.js:

/** * @typedef {import('./types').User} User */ /** * @param {User} */
function foo(user) {}

No type inference or intellisense

Some people seem to think that using JSDoc somehow will cause you to lose type inference. As already demonstrated earlier above, this is also not true. Consider the following example:

The reason for this claim seems to be that people don't understand that when you're using JSDoc for types, you're still using typescript. TypeScript is still doing the typechecking.

Manually writing types is bothersome

Some people claimed that writing types manually is bothersome. I can only assume that this is a case of preference, or perhaps its not clear to those people that you can still .d.ts files. Some people will prefer example B over example A. This is fine. Both can be used when using JSDoc for types.

example A:

/** * @typedef {Object} User * @property {string} username * @property {number} age */

example B:

export interface User { username: string, age: number
}

But that still uses TypeScript!

Yes, this is the point.

In conclusion

Finally, and I'm repeating myself here, using TypeScript without compiling your code is a preference. There is no right or wrong answer and I challenge anyone who is skeptical of this approach to be a little bit more open minded and give it a try some time when you're starting a new project, you might find it's actually a quite nice approach of utilizing types. And if you end up not liking it, that's fine too!
item.author: thepassle

item.title: 用于 TDD 的 GPT4 CLI：您编写测试，GPT 编写代码使其通过 ✅ | GPT4 CLI for TDD: you write the test, GPT writes the code to pass it ✅
item.description:
迪马·苏哈列夫

发表于 3 月 27 日

您编写测试，GPT 编写代码使其通过 ✅。

是的，我知道。 这是一个简单但绝妙的想法和千禧年的开源。 所以请随意分叉和功能。

大概就是这样。

我不会告诉你“为什么以及如何测试软件是一种祝福”的全部 10 条诫命，只有一件事——测试就像你的朋友，他们告诉你你想听什么，但你只能相信他们，他们无论如何节省你的$$。

使用 AI-TDD，您可以调整测试，然后为您编写代码。 您离开“如何编写此功能”的低级别并移至“功能应该做什么”的级别。 你从“实施”中清醒过来，开始以专业的高级工程师的身份操作想法。

由于AITDD是开源的，我很乐意合并您的 PR，所以请随意分叉和添加功能！

还有一个很好的工具——OpenCommit——一个 GPT CLI，可以在 1 秒内自动生成常规提交。

我可能会通过其他非凡的发明和启蒙来改变世界，目前还不确定，但无论如何，请关注 Twitter 帐户twitter.com/io_Y_oi （或不关注）。

一旦暂停，disukharev 将无法发表评论或发布帖子，直到他们的暂停被解除。

解除暂停后，disukharev 将能够再次发表评论和发布帖子。

一旦取消发布，disukharev 的所有帖子都将隐藏起来，只有他们自己可以访问。

如果 disukharev 没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从他们的仪表板重新发布他们的帖子。

笔记：

感谢您维护 DEV 社区的安全。 您可以通过以下方式标记 disukharev：

降低 disukharev 的所有帖子的可见度

报告其他不当行为

取消标记 disukharev 将恢复其帖子的默认可见性。



原文:

Dima Sukharev

Posted on Mar 27

   

AI-TDD — GPT4 CLI for TDD. You write the test, GPT writes the code to pass it ✅.

Yes, I know. It's a simple yet brilliant idea and open-source of the millennium. So feel free to fork and feature.

— Why? — TDD is the only right way of.. — Wowow, man.. wait.. really.. don't.. — (Hits vape): Ok, ok... GPTs are not so good when prompted to generate code from a free text input, BUT when you prompt it with a test — GPT4 is killing it with impressive accuracy.

— Hmm.. And the more clearly you formulate a test, the cleaner and wiser you write the code?

This is probably it.

I won't tell you the whole 10 commandments of "why and how testing software is a blessing", just one thing — tests are like your friends, they tell you what you want to hear, but you can only trust them, and they save your a$$ anyway.

With AI-TDD, you adjust the tests, and the code is written for you. You leave the low level of "how to code this feature" and move above to the level of "what the feature should do". You clean your head from "implementations" and start operating ideas as the pro maximum deluxe engineer.

As AITDD is open-sourced, I'm happy to merge your PRs, so feel free to fork and feature!

There is one more good tool — OpenCommit — a GPT CLI to auto-generate conventional commits in 1 second.

I'll probably change the world with other extraordinary inventions and enlightenment, not sure yet, but anyway, follow the Twitter account twitter.com/io_Y_oi (or don't).

Once suspended, disukharev will not be able to comment or publish posts until their suspension is removed.

Once unsuspended, disukharev will be able to comment and publish posts again.

Once unpublished, all posts by disukharev will become hidden and only accessible to themselves.

If disukharev is not suspended, they can still re-publish their posts from their dashboard.

Note:

Thanks for keeping DEV Community safe. Here is what you can do to flag disukharev:

Make all posts by disukharev less visible

disukharev consistently posts content that violates DEV Community's code of conduct because it is harassing, offensive or spammy.

Report other inappropriate conduct

Unflagging disukharev will restore default visibility to their posts.
item.author: disukharev

item.title: OpenCommit：GPT CLI 可在 1 秒内自动生成令人印象深刻的提交🤯🔫 | OpenCommit: GPT CLI to auto-generate impressive commits in 1 second 🤯🔫
item.description:
迪马·苏哈列夫

发表于 3 月 27 日

首先——让我们发展我的推特，这样我就可以更快地讲述我的发明，让世界变得更美好（或者不是，idk）

自 2017 年以来，我一直在努力制作提交消息。

当您想到提交消息时——您会从编写代码切换上下文。 当你切换上下文时——你是在浪费时间。 浪费时间是蹩脚的。

我通常会进行 1 行提交，例如“amends”、“fixed bug”、“refactoring”、“refactoring 2”、“final refactoring”，然后在将分支合并到生产环境时尝试提出一个好的通用信息。

它随着第一次人工智能革命而改变 :)) 我的意思是所有这些 GPT..

两周前，我开源了OpenCommit 。

该库使用传统的提交约定，这使得提交有意义。 如果愿意，您也可以打开GitMoji设置。 并选择不同的语言和许多其他功能（到目前为止我获得了 12 个 PR）

只需使用 npm 安装它：

安装 type oc并在终端中点击“Enter”后：

如果您希望 IDE 使用 OpenCommit，请执行以下操作：

就是这样。

有关更多功能，请阅读文档。

你可以关注我的推特，也可以不关注

一旦暂停，disukharev 将无法发表评论或发布帖子，直到他们的暂停被解除。

解除暂停后，disukharev 将能够再次发表评论和发布帖子。

一旦取消发布，disukharev 的所有帖子都将隐藏起来，只有他们自己可以访问。

如果 disukharev 没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从他们的仪表板重新发布他们的帖子。

笔记：

感谢您维护 DEV 社区的安全。 您可以通过以下方式标记 disukharev：

降低 disukharev 的所有帖子的可见度

报告其他不当行为

取消标记 disukharev 将恢复其帖子的默认可见性。



原文:

Dima Sukharev

Posted on Mar 27

   

first — let's grow my twitter, so i can tell about my inventions quicker and make the world even better place (or not, idk)

I've been struggling making commit messages since 2017.

When you think of a commit message — you switch context from writing code. When you switch context — you waste time. Wasting time is lame.

I usually made 1 line commits like "amends", "fixed bug", "refactoring", "refactoring 2", "final refactoring" and then tried to come up with a good general message when merging a branch to production.

It has changed with THE FIRST AI REVOLUTION :)) I mean all this GPTs..

Two weeks ago I open-sourced OpenCommit.

The lib uses conventional commits convention which makes commits meaningful. You can also turn on GitMoji setting if you like. And select different languages and many other features (I got 12 PRs so far)

Simply install it with npm:

After installing type oc and hit "Enter" in a terminal:

If you want OpenCommit to be used by your IDE do:

That's it.

For more features read the docs.

you may follow my twitter or not

item.title: AWS 开源通讯，#150 | AWS open source newsletter, #150
item.description:
欢迎

大家好，欢迎阅读里程碑版的 AWS 开源时事通讯 #150。 超过 20 万字之后，成千上万的贡献者，数百个新的开源项目，我希望这份时事通讯能为读者带来和我将其放在一起一样多的快乐。 感谢大家迄今为止的大力支持。 本周我们为您准备了什么？ 是的，更多很棒的新项目和内容。 本周我们有这样的项目，例如“ZeusCloud”，一个用于风险管理的开源解决方案，“balcony”，一个很好的 cli 向导工具，“emr-trino-autoscale”，为那些运行 Trino 的人，“amazon-kinesis-data-analytics- blueprints” 为 Apache Flink 精心挑选的现成模式，等等！ 此外，还有一些您最喜欢的开源项目，包括 Apache Hudi、Apache Iceberg、Kubernetes、FFMpeg、Apache Airflow、Protobuf、MySQL、MariaDB、Amazon Corretto、Delta Lake 等。

在深入阅读时事通讯之前，请花点时间阅读 Lukonde Mwila 和 Chris Short 为那些运行 Kubernetes 工作负载的人撰写的这篇文章。

行动：如果您使用 Kubernetes，请阅读此内容

我之前分享过这个，但现在我的同事 Lukonde Mwila 和 Chris Short 将这篇文章放在一起，对 Kubernetes 容器镜像注册表的更改，以提高人们对即将发生的 Kubernetes 镜像注册表的一些关键更改的认识，您需要审查和行动。 该帖子提供了一些关于您需要做什么的提示，所以请帮自己一个忙，并确保在跳过之前阅读此内容。

反馈

请花 1 分钟时间完成这个简短的调查，并获得一些独家内容作为感谢。

庆祝开源贡献者

多亏了众多开源贡献者，本期通讯中分享的文章和项目才有可能实现。 我想大声喊出并感谢那些真正推动开源并使我们所有人都能在他们所创造的基础上学习和构建的人。

感谢以下开源英雄：Ivica Kolenkaš、Franck Pachot、Guillaume Marchand、Daisy Riley、Oğuzhan Yılmaz、Veena Vasudevan、Soumil Shah、Sashi Varanasi、Lakshmi Peri、Noritaka Sekiyama、Scott Long、Sean Ma、Surendar Munimohan、Chelluru VNSS Vidyadhar、José Gardiazabal 和 Donato Azevedo

最新的开源项目

开源项目的好处在于您可以查看源代码。 如果您喜欢这些项目的外观，请务必查看代码，如果它对您有用，请与维护者联系以提供反馈、建议甚至提交贡献。

工具

宙斯云

它有什么作用？ 根据 README，它：构建您的 AWS 账户的资产清单，持续监控您的环境是否存在错误配置和攻击路径，让您自定义安全性和合规性控制以满足您的需求，提供上下文信息以帮助您确定安全发现的优先级和补救措施，并帮助您满足 PCI DSS、CIS 等合规性标准！

检查回购以获得演示 gif 的外观。

阳台

使用它可以毫不费力地自动管理您账户中的 AWS 资源。 我安装了它并在几分钟内启动并运行，使用优秀的文档让我开始。 自述文件中有大量示例说明如何使用此工具，还有一个非常好的文档站点，其中包含示例食谱。

通过他的脚本运行您的 cron 设置，它将有助于输出示例以确保它是您所期望的。 文档包含大量示例，但非常适合在将 crontab 放在一起之前进行测试。

可视化 iam-access-analyzer-policy-validation-findings

该存储库分享了一篇支持性的博客文章，以帮助您开始部署该项目。

该实用程序可以安装在外部 EC2 实例上，也可以安装在您要控制的集群的 Amazon EMR 主节点上。 该软件包支持开箱即用的实例组和具有按需/SPOT 实例的实例队列集群。 查看文档以了解该项目提供的功能，例如基于集群 CPU 利用率的缩放、使用 Trino JMX REST API 的指标收集、使用 Trino required_workers SESSION 参数的缩放提示等等！

演示、示例、解决方案和研讨会

亚马逊运动数据分析蓝图

每个蓝图将带您了解如何使用 Apache Flink 解决与流处理相关的实际问题。 这些蓝图可用于创建更复杂的应用程序，以解决您在 Apache Flink 中的业务挑战。

该堆栈将部署所需的基础设施以及使用 Amazon ECS 和 Fargate 部署 Drupal。

为了使其超级简单，rhlarora84 将其打包为 CDK 应用程序。 设置完成后，您可以像发送 SQS 消息一样简单地启动 ECS 任务。

库伯内斯

我已经精选了 Franck Pachot 之前的 Kubernetes 博文，这次他又带来了他的 Kubernetes on AWS 系列的另一篇文章。 在这篇文章中，他深入探讨了 Linux 内核的一个强大但鲜为人知的特性：压力失速信息 (PSI) 以及如何使用它来了解工作负载中的容量和资源模式。 阿帕奇气流

本周我在电子纸上写下了一个非常酷的开源项目，名为 Data on EKS（或 DoEKS），它为许多常见的开源数据工具的安装提供了参考模式。 在这篇短文中，自我管理的 Apache Airflow with Data on EKS ，我分享了我启动和运行它的经验（包括当它出现可怕错误时的混乱部分！）。 我自己多次使用它来创建延时视频，从我的 Raspberry Pi 捕获帧。 在博文“使用 AWS Batch 为您的媒体作业创建托管 FFmpeg 工作流”中，Guillaume Marchand 和 Daisy Riley 向您展示了如何将 FFmpeg 与 AWS 服务集成以构建更易于管理的 FFmpeg。 他们创建了一个开源解决方案来部署打包在容器中并由 AWS Batch 管理的 FFmpeg，然后您可以通过 REST API 将 FFmpeg 命令作为作业执行。 很不错！ 其他帖子和快速阅读

快速更新

亚马逊 Corretto 20

您现在可以从Amazon Corretto 下载页面下载 Corretto 20。 这个最新版本支持最新的 OpenJDK 功能版本，可在 Linux、Windows 和 macOS 上使用。

您可以一起使用记录模式和类型模式来创建更强大的数据导航。 虚拟线程也处于第二个预览版中。 虚拟线程将使编写多线程应用程序变得更加容易。 对 switch 语句预览功能的模式匹配和孵化向量 API 进行了更新。 数据库

有关此版本的更多详细信息，请参阅 PostgreSQL 社区公告。 您还可以为您的代理强制执行基于 SCRAM（加盐质询响应身份验证机制）密码的身份验证。

我最近分享了 AWS 如何支持来自 .NET 社区的越来越多的开源项目。 在这条推文中，我们分享了最新的项目 Nuke。 请务必在此处查看完整列表

本周视频

深入了解 AWS 上的 Apache Iceberg

在本视频中，Veena Vasudevan 探讨了在本地使用传统文件格式的一些常见挑战，以及如何利用 AWS 上的 Apache Iceberg 帮助您克服这些挑战。 您还将通过精心制作的演示了解 Apache Iceberg 的全面和高级功能，这些演示展示了 Apache Iceberg on AWS 的独特功能。

阿帕奇胡迪

我从 Soumil Shah 发现了一系列新的短视频，其中介绍了如何利用 AWS 上的 Apache Hudi 构建开源数据湖。 涵盖了很多主题，这对技术观众来说是完美的。

在此处查看完整的播放列表，开幕式将留给您。

以开源为基础

对于那些不熟悉这个节目的人，Build on Open Source 是我们回顾这个时事通讯的地方，然后邀请特邀嘉宾深入研究他们的开源项目。 期待大量的代码、演示和希望的笑声。 我们整理了一个播放列表，以便您可以轻松访问 Build on Open Source 节目的所有（八）集。 基于开源播放列表

如果您计划在 2023 年举办任何活动，无论是虚拟活动、面对面活动还是混合活动，请与我们联系，因为我很乐意与读者分享您活动的详细信息。

以开源为基础
福萨西亚
如果您有兴趣亲自或以虚拟方式参加，请在FOSSASIA 峰会 2023 页面上了解有关该活动的更多信息。

该活动由社区组织 - 为社区服务。 查看完整的活动详细信息并在此处节省您的空间， AWS Community Nordics 注册页面

降低 openCypher 应用程序的成本
与峰值容量配置相比，使用 Neptune 无服务器，客户可以节省高达 90% 的成本。 本次会议将包括 Neptune 的实际演示。

前往 You Tube 持有页面，降低 openCypher 应用程序的成本

皮层
每隔一个星期四，下一个星期四是 2 月 16 日

您可以查看 GitHub 项目了解更多详情，前往社区会议部分。 社区电话会保留以前会议的滚动文档，因此您可以赶上以前的讨论。 查看Cortex 社区会议记录了解更多信息。

打开搜索
每隔一个星期二，格林威治标准时间下午 3 点

此定期聚会面向所有对 OpenSearch 和 Open Distro 感兴趣的人。 欢迎所有技能水平的人，他们涵盖并欢迎讨论以下主题：搜索、日志记录、日志分析和数据可视化。

报名参加下一次会议， OpenSearch 社区会议

与 AWS 的开源保持联系

请记得查看开源主页，通过在@AWSOpen上关注我们，了解我们在开源领域的所有活动的最新信息



原文:

Welcome

Hello and welcome to a milestone edition of the AWS open source newsletter, #150. Over two hundred thousand words later, thousands of contributors, hundreds of new open source projects, I hope this newsletter brings as much joy for readers as it does for me to put this together. Thank you all for your amazing support so far. What do we have in store for you this week? Yup, more great new projects and content. This week we have projects such "ZeusCloud" an open source solution for risk management, "balcony" a nice tool for cli wizards, "emr-trino-autoscale" for those of you running Trino, "amazon-kinesis-data-analytics-blueprints" a nice selection of ready made patterns for Apache Flink, and more! Also featured are some of your favourite open source projects, including Apache Hudi, Apache Iceberg, Kubernetes, FFMpeg, Apache Airflow, Protobuf, MySQL, MariaDB, Amazon Corretto, Delta Lake, and more.

Before diving into the newsletter, take a moment to read this post from Lukonde Mwila and Chris Short for those of you running Kubernetes workloads.

ACTION: Read this if you use Kubernetes

I have shared this before, but now my colleagues Lukonde Mwila and Chris Short have put together this post, Changes to the Kubernetes Container Image Registry to raise awareness of some critical changes to the Kubernetes image registries that are happening very soon and that you need to review and action. The post provides some tips on what you need to do, so do yourself a favour and make sure you read this before skipping on.

Feedback

Please please please take 1 minute to complete this short survey and get some exclusive content as a thank you.

Celebrating open source contributors

The articles and projects shared in this newsletter are only possible thanks to the many contributors in open source. I would like to shout out and thank those folks who really do power open source and enable us all to learn and build on top of what they have created.

So thank you to the following open source heroes: Ivica Kolenkaš, Franck Pachot, Guillaume Marchand, Daisy Riley, Oğuzhan Yılmaz, Veena Vasudevan, Soumil Shah, Sashi Varanasi, Lakshmi Peri, Noritaka Sekiyama, Scott Long, Sean Ma, Surendar Munimohan, Chelluru V N S S Vidyadhar, José Gardiazabal and Donato Azevedo

Latest open source projects

The great thing about open source projects is that you can review the source code. If you like the look of these projects, make sure you that take a look at the code, and if it is useful to you, get in touch with the maintainer to provide feedback, suggestions or even submit a contribution.

Tools

ZeusCloud

ZeusCloud is a new (to me at least) tool to help you discover, prioritise, and remediate your risks in the cloud. What does it do? Well according to the README, it: builds an asset inventory of your AWS accounts, continuously monitors your environments for misconfigurations and attack paths, lets you customise security and compliance controls to fit your needs, provides contextual information to help you prioritise and remediate security findings, and helps you to meet compliance standards such as PCI DSS, CIS, and more!

Check the repo for demo gifs of what this looks like.

balcony

balcony is an open source Python based CLI tool from Oğuzhan Yılmaz for cli wizards and fans, that simplifies the process of enumerating AWS resources. Use it to effortlessly automate the managed of the AWS resources in your account. I installed it and was up and running in minutes, using the excellent documentation to get me going. The README has plenty of examples of how you can use this tool, as well as a super nice documentation site complete with sample recipes.

cronrange.info

cronrange.info this project is not AWS specific, but I love what Ivica Kolenkaš has put together here - the essential guide to undersanding cron patterns. Run your cron settings through his script, and it will helpfully output samples to make sure it is what you expect. The docs contain plenty of examples, but great for testing out before you put your crontab together.

visualize-iam-access-analyzer-policy-validation-findings

visualize-iam-access-analyzer-policy-validation-findings provides code that helps you build a useful dashboard in Quicksight to visualise the policy validation findings from AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) Access Analyzer. The repo shares a supporting blog post to help you get started deploying this project.

emr-trino-autoscale

emr-trino-autoscale This project provides a custom auto-autoscaling for Amazon EMR on EC2 clusters running with Trino. The utility can be installed on external EC2 instances or on the Amazon EMR master of the cluster you want to control. The package support out of the box Instance Groups and Instance Fleets clusters with On Demand / SPOT instances. Check out the doc for features this project provides, such as scaling based on cluster CPU utilisation, metrics collection using Trino JMX REST API, scaling hints using Trino required_workers SESSION parameter, and more!

aws-glue-connector-google-spreadsheet

aws-glue-connector-google-spreadsheet This repository provides a tutorial on how to use AWS Glue for Python Shell to access your Data in Google Spreadsheet and import it to your Amazon S3 bucket.

Demos, Samples, Solutions and Workshops

amazon-kinesis-data-analytics-blueprints

amazon-kinesis-data-analytics-blueprints are a curated collection of Apache Flink applications. Each blueprint will walk you through how to solve a practical problem related to stream processing using Apache Flink. These blueprints can be leveraged to create more complex applications to solve your business challenges in Apache Flink.

aws-cdk-localgov-drupal-fargate-efs-auroraserverlessv2

aws-cdk-localgov-drupal-fargate-efs-auroraserverlessv2 provides a nice way to automate the deployment of Drupal using AWS CDK. The stack will deploy the infrastructure needed as well as deploying Drupal using Amazon ECS and Fargate.

sqs-fargate-eventbridge-pipe

sqs-fargate-eventbridge-pipe provides a sample for how to invoke a Fargate Task from SQS using Eventbridge Pipes. To make it super simple, rhlarora84 has packaged this up as a CDK app. Once setup, you can kick off your ECS Task as simple as sending an SQS message.

AWS and Community blog posts

Kubernetes

I have featured previous Kubernetes posts from Franck Pachot, and this time he is back with another instalment of his Kubernetes on AWS series. In this post, he dives deep into a powerful but less known feature of the Linux Kernel: Pressure Stall Information (PSI) and how you can use this to understand capacity and resource patterns in your workloads. [hands on]

Apache Airflow

I put pen to digital paper this week and wrote about a very cool open source project called Data on EKS (or DoEKS) which provides reference patterns for the installation of many common open source data tools. In this short post, Self managed Apache Airflow with Data on EKS, I share my experiences of getting this up and running (including the messy bits when it goes horribly wrong!). [hands on]

FFmpeg

FFmpeg is an open source and widely used utility for handling video. I have used it myself many times to create time lapse videos capturing frames from my Raspberry Pi. In the post, Create a Managed FFmpeg Workflow for Your Media Jobs Using AWS Batch, Guillaume Marchand and Daisy Riley show you how to integrate FFmpeg with AWS Services to build a more easily managed FFmpeg. They have created an open source solution to deploy FFmpeg packaged in a container and managed by AWS Batch, which you can then execute an FFmpeg command as a job through a REST API. Very nice! [hands on]

Other posts and quick reads

Quick updates

Amazon Corretto 20

You can now download Corretto 20 from the Amazon Corretto downloads page. This latest version supports the most recent OpenJDK feature release and is available on Linux, Windows, and macOS.

Highlights of OpenJDK 20 include a second preview of Record Patterns, which are used to more easily work with record-based objects. You can use record patterns and type patterns together to create more powerful data navigation. Virtual threads are also in their second preview. Virtual Threads will make it easier to write multi-threaded applications. OpenJDK 20 also introduces a new incubation feature for scoped values, which allows you to share data between threads. OpenJDK 20 includes a second preview of the Foreign Function & Memory API, which makes it easier to integrate with native code. There are updates to the Pattern Matching for switch statements preview feature and the incubating Vector API. Structured Concurrency, which makes tasks distributed over multiple threads appear as a single unit of work, is now in its second incubation release.

PostgreSQL

Amazon Relational Database Service (RDS) Proxy is a fully managed and highly available database proxy for Aurora and RDS databases. RDS Proxy helps improve application scalability, resiliency, and security. RDS Proxy now supports PostgreSQL major version 15. PostgreSQL 15 includes new features such as the SQL standard "MERGE" command for conditional SQL queries, performance improvements for both in-memory and disk-based sorting, support for two-phase commit, and row/column filtering for logical replication. The PostgreSQL 15 release also adds support for server-side compression with Gzip, LZ4, or Zstandard (zstd) using pg_basebackup. For more details about this release, refer to the PostgreSQL community announcement. You can also enforce SCRAM (Salted Challenge Response Authentication Mechanism) password-based authentication for your proxy.

.NET on AWS

I shared recently how AWS is supporting the growing number of open source projects from the .NET community. In this tweet we share the latest project, Nuke. Make sure you check out the full list here

Videos of the week

Dive deep of Apache Iceberg on AWS

In this video, Veena Vasudevan looks at some of the common challenges of using traditional file formats on premises and how leveraging Apache Iceberg on AWS helps you overcome these challenges. You will also learn about the comprehensive and advanced features of Apache Iceberg with elaborate demos that showcase the unique capabilities of Apache Iceberg on AWS.

Apache Hudi

I discovered a new series of short videos from Soumil Shah that looks at how you can leverage Apache Hudi on AWS to build an open source data lake. There are lots of topics covered, and this is perfect for the technical viewer.

Check out the full playlist here and will leave you with the opening session.

Build on Open Source

For those unfamiliar with this show, Build on Open Source is where we go over this newsletter and then invite special guests to dive deep into their open source project. Expect plenty of code, demos and hopefully laughs. We have put together a playlist so that you can easily access all (eight) of the episodes of the Build on Open Source show. Build on Open Source playlist

If you are planning any events in 2023, either virtual, in person, or hybrid, get in touch as I would love to share details of your event with readers.

Build on Open Source
March 31st, twitch.tv/aws

The fourth episode of Build on Open Source features special guests Devansh Bawari and Saurav Pathak from Bagisto, a leading open source e-commerce technology. See you there on twitch.tv/aws, Friday 31st March at 9am GMT, 10am CET.

FOSSASIA
April 13th-15th, Singapore

FOSSASIA Summit 2023 returns as an in-person and online event, taking place from Thursday 13th April to Saturday 15th April at the Lifelong Learning Institute in Singapore.

If you are interested in attending in person, or virtually, find out more about the event at the FOSSASIA Summit 2023 page.

AWS Community Nordics
April, 20th Helsinki

The AWS Community Day Nordics is a free full day event for AWS users to come together to network, learn from each other and get inspired. The event is organised by the community - for the community. The cfp is currently open, so if you are in the area and want to talk then here is your chance. Check out the full event details and save your space here, AWS Community Nordics registration page

Reducing the costs of your openCypher applications
May 8th, 4pm UK - online

openCypher is an open-source project for creating graph applications. Neptune supports openCypher graph query language, and in this webinar you will learn more about the cost benefits for moving openCypher workloads to Neptune serverless. With Neptune serverless, customers can see up to 90% cost savings compared to provisioning for peak capacity. A demo of Neptune in action will be included in this session.

Head over to the You Tube holding page, Reducing the costs of your openCypher applications

Cortex
Every other Thursday, next one 16th February

The Cortex community call happens every two weeks on Thursday, alternating at 1200 UTC and 1700 UTC. You can check out the GitHub project for more details, go to the Community Meetings section. The community calls keep a rolling doc of previous meetings, so you can catch up on the previous discussions. Check the Cortex Community Meetings Notes for more info.

OpenSearch
Every other Tuesday, 3pm GMT

This regular meet-up is for anyone interested in OpenSearch & Open Distro. All skill levels are welcome and they cover and welcome talks on topics including: search, logging, log analytics, and data visualisation.

Sign up to the next session, OpenSearch Community Meeting

Stay in touch with open source at AWS

Remember to check out the Open Source homepage to keep up to date with all our activity in open source by following us on @AWSOpen

item.title: 一个灵活的nodejs爬虫库——x-crawl | A flexible nodejs crawler library —— x-crawl
item.description:
编码器HXL

发表于 3 月 27 日

可以批量抓取页面、批量网络请求、批量下载文件资源、轮询抓取等。支持异步/同步模式抓取。 运行在nodejs上，使用灵活简单，对JS/TS开发者友好。

如果觉得不错，可以给x-crawl仓库一个Star支持一下，你的Star会是我更新的动力。

  • 例子

    定时抓拍：以每天自动抓拍Airbnb Plus房源封面图为例：

     // 1.Import module ES/CJS import xCrawl from 'x-crawl' // 2.Create a crawler instance const myXCrawl = xCrawl({ timeout: 10000, // overtime time intervalTime: { max: 3000, min: 2000 } // crawl interval }) // 3.Set the crawling task /* Call the startPolling API to start the polling function, and the callback function will be called every other day */ myXCrawl.startPolling({ d: 1 }, async (count, stopPolling) => { // Call crawlPage API to crawl Page const { page } = await myXCrawl.crawlPage('https://zh.airbnb.com/s/*/plus_homes') // set request configuration const plusBoxHandle = await page.$('.a1stauiv') const requestConfig = await plusBoxHandle!.$$eval('picture img', (imgEls) => { return imgEls.map((item) => item.src) }) // Call the crawlFile API to crawl pictures myXCrawl.crawlFile({ requestConfig, fileConfig: { storeDir: './upload' } }) // Close page page.close() })

    运行结果：

    注意：不要随意抓取，抓取前可以查看robots.txt协议。 这只是为了演示如何使用 x-crawl。

    更多的

    更详细的文档请查看： github.com/coder-hxl/x-crawl

一旦暂停，coderhxl 将无法评论或发布帖子，直到他们的暂停被删除。

解除暂停后，coderhxl 将能够再次发表评论和发布帖子。

取消发布后，coderhxl 的所有帖子都将隐藏起来，只有他们自己可以访问。

如果 coderhxl 没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从他们的仪表板重新发布他们的帖子。

笔记：

感谢您维护 DEV 社区的安全。 以下是您可以用来标记 coderhxl 的操作：

降低 coderhxl 的所有帖子的可见度

报告其他不当行为

取消标记 coderhxl 将恢复其帖子的默认可见性。



原文:

CoderHXL

Posted on Mar 27

   

x-crawl is a flexible nodejs crawler library. It can crawl pages in batches, network requests in batches, download file resources in batches, polling and crawling, etc. Supports asynchronous/synchronous mode crawling. Running on nodejs, the usage is flexible and simple, friendly to JS/TS developers.

If you feel good, you can give x-crawl repository a Star to support it, your Star will be the motivation for my update.

  • 🔥 Asynchronous/Synchronous - Support asynchronous/synchronous mode batch crawling.
  • ⚙️ Multiple functions - Batch crawling of pages, batch network requests, batch download of file resources, polling crawling, etc.
  • 🖋️ Flexible writing style - Multiple crawling configurations and ways to get crawling results.
  • ⏱️ Interval crawling - no interval/fixed interval/random interval, you can use/avoid high concurrent crawling.
  • ☁️ Crawl SPA - Batch crawl SPA (Single Page Application) to generate pre-rendered content (ie "SSR" (Server Side Rendering)).
  • ⚒️ Controlling Pages - Headless browsers can submit forms, keystrokes, event actions, generate screenshots of pages, etc.
  • 🧾 Capture Record - Capture and record the crawled results, and highlight the reminders.
  • 🦾TypeScript - Own types, implement complete types through generics.

Example

Timing capture: Take the automatic capture of the cover image of Airbnb Plus listings every day as an example:

// 1.Import module ES/CJS
import xCrawl from 'x-crawl' // 2.Create a crawler instance
const myXCrawl = xCrawl({ timeout: 10000, // overtime time intervalTime: { max: 3000, min: 2000 } // crawl interval
}) // 3.Set the crawling task
/* Call the startPolling API to start the polling function, and the callback function will be called every other day
*/
myXCrawl.startPolling({ d: 1 }, async (count, stopPolling) => { // Call crawlPage API to crawl Page const { page } = await myXCrawl.crawlPage('https://zh.airbnb.com/s/*/plus_homes') // set request configuration const plusBoxHandle = await page.$('.a1stauiv') const requestConfig = await plusBoxHandle!.$$eval('picture img', (imgEls) => { return imgEls.map((item) => item.src) }) // Call the crawlFile API to crawl pictures myXCrawl.crawlFile({ requestConfig, fileConfig: { storeDir: './upload' } }) // Close page page.close()
})

running result:

Note: Do not crawl at will, you can check the robots.txt protocol before crawling. This is just to demonstrate how to use x-crawl.

More

For more detailed documentation, please check: github.com/coder-hxl/x-crawl

Once suspended, coderhxl will not be able to comment or publish posts until their suspension is removed.

Once unsuspended, coderhxl will be able to comment and publish posts again.

Once unpublished, all posts by coderhxl will become hidden and only accessible to themselves.

If coderhxl is not suspended, they can still re-publish their posts from their dashboard.

Note:

Thanks for keeping DEV Community safe. Here is what you can do to flag coderhxl:

Make all posts by coderhxl less visible

coderhxl consistently posts content that violates DEV Community's code of conduct because it is harassing, offensive or spammy.

Report other inappropriate conduct

Unflagging coderhxl will restore default visibility to their posts.
item.author: coderhxl

item.title: 为亚马逊编码是什么感觉（结论） | What It Was Like To Code For Amazon (Conclusion)
item.description:
如果您阅读过本系列的前两篇文章中的任何一篇，您就会知道我在亚马逊的体验是……次优的。 但我必须承认的第一件事是，我实际上并不知道为 Amazon 编写代码一般是什么感觉。 事实上，即使您过去曾为 Amazon 编写代码，或者即使您现在正在为他们编写代码，您也不知道为他们编写代码一般是什么感觉。 公司太大了，太多的开发人员分散在无数的团队和地点，以至于任何一个人都无法权威地说出整体开发体验到底是什么样的。

在这么大的公司里，你真正能做的就是评估自己的经历。 有些人可能有过与我相似的经历——甚至更糟。 但我也确信一些亚马逊编码员在那里非常开心。 在某种程度上，这完全取决于您在哪个团队/小组中工作，您究竟为谁工作/与谁一起工作，当然还有您为体验带来了什么。

但是，虽然我无法准确描绘出亚马逊任何团队/团队中任何人的具体情况，但我确实看到了足够多的东西来得出一些奇怪的结论。

口头上的“原则”

亚马逊不断吹捧他们的“领导原则”。 当你面试的时候，他们会问你关于他们的事情。 他们在公司会议上谈论它们。 至少从表面上看，他们为这些原则倾注了大量精力。 不幸的是，很多这种强调都是空谈。 毫无疑问，我的观点受到我自己的负面经历和我自己（公认的短暂）任期的影响。 但根据我的经验，完全失败的“原则”最明显的例子是：

有骨干； 不同意和承诺
领导者有义务在他们不同意时恭敬地质疑决定，即使这样做会让人感到不舒服或筋疲力尽。 领导者有信念，有毅力。 他们不会为了社会凝聚力而妥协。 一旦做出决定，他们就会全力以赴。


我看到太多的例子使这个原则成为一个彻头彻尾的笑话，我什至不能在这里一一列举。 我不只是在谈论我的经历。 我说的是我在组织其他领域目睹的事情，以及其他员工直接告诉我的事情。

我亲眼目睹了很多人们根本不敢说出来的例子。 我参加的会议中，除了“会说话的人”之外，没有人敢发表任何反对意见。 当他们确实费心提出问题时，我也看到了这些问题被推翻的地方，因为没有其他选择可以让管理层满意。

一个有说服力的观察

当我写这篇关于在 Amazon 工作的文章时 ( dev.to/bytebodger/what-its-like-to-code ...)，来自公司完全不同部门的一位高级开发人员在 Slack 上联系了我。 他称赞了这篇文章，感谢我写了这篇文章，并说他很高兴看到我相处得很好。

我感谢他的反馈。 但我也告诉他，在我写这篇文章后的这段时间里，我遇到了一些非常困难的情况。 他是澳大利亚人。 他告诉我，在他之前的工作中，开发团队多次讨论已经构建的应用程序的某些特定部分。 他们经常会发现自己对彼此说：“是的……这有点废话，不是吗？”

但他也告诉我，当他加入亚马逊时，他遇到了非常相似的问题。 因为，在他被录用后的很长一段时间里，没有人愿意听到任何不同意见——即使他们只是“反对”作为讨论问题和（希望）找到更好解决方案的一种方式。

他的具体建议是暂时保持低调。 不多说。 只是有点“同意”你口述的任何事情。 然后，只有当你已经在船上工作了一段时间之后，才有可能谈论一些有针对性的事情。

明确地说，我明白了。 我会是第一个告诉你的人，没有人希望这个全新的人在第一天出现，然后立即告诉所有人他们一直在做糟糕的工作。 而且旧版应用程序很糟糕。 并且他们当前的所有做法都应该被扔到窗外。

但是，成为一个傲慢的万事通与仅仅成为……谦虚但自信的团队成员之间是有区别的。 有时一些最好的建议来自新人。 因为其他人都已经习惯了“一贯的做法”。 只要新来的人不是自大的混蛋，了解某人如何以全新的眼光看待情况就很有价值。

这就是有骨干的东西； 不同意和承诺应该是关于。 如果你看到一些似乎“不对劲”的东西……说点什么。 与您的同事讨论。 或者你的经理。 或者你的利益相关者。

有时，当您提出这些担忧时，您会被“否决”。 这很好。 其他时候，您可能会发现人们实际上同意您的观点——但还有其他一些实际原因会继续以这种方式进行。 再一次 -没关系。 但至少进行这些对话要健康得多，而不是仅仅因为你提出了合理的担忧而害怕你会被贴上不满的标签。

大哥心态

这是我在亚马逊多次遇到的事情。 我发现它有点……令人不安。

我会和 Slack 上的某个人聊一些看起来“不对劲”的事情。 也许是关于一个项目。 或者关于一个特定的人。 甚至是关于公司的总体情况。 在这些场景中，有多个不同的人，当另一个人输入以下内容时，Slack 对话将结束：

也许我们应该删除这个 Slack 对话？


现在要明确一点，我们不是在谈论轰炸首席执行官的房子。 我们没有称任何人为混蛋。 事实上，我们并没有在那些 Slack 线程中讨论任何我不敢直接当着他们的面对其他人说的事情。 我们只是在讨论我们遇到的问题，以及可能如何解决这些问题。

然而，在很多情况下，我的 Slack 对手会通过建议我们删除线程来结束对话。 想想…… 老实说，每次遇到都觉得挺心寒的。

明目张胆的背叛

在编写了四分之一个世纪之后，我已经记不清有多少同事根本不喜欢我了。 在足够多的公司工作，有足够多的同事，有些人根本不会“接受”你，这是不可避免的。 但这不是我在这里谈论的。

尽管我在整个职业生涯中经历过各种人际冲突，但只有一家公司让我经历过公开的背后捅刀子。 当然，那家公司就是亚马逊。

当我说“背后捅刀子”时，我并不是指有人说我是个混蛋（拿一个数字——这是一条很长的线）。 我也不是指有人说我的代码、我的可交付成果或任何关于我的工作努力的坏话。 我特别指的是有人当着我的面说我做得很好。 然后转身，每当一个项目遇到任何挫折，并告诉其他人我把一切都搞砸了。

有人当面告诉我，他们认为我真的搞砸了。 老实说，一旦你克服了那些时刻的原始情绪，有时这些互动就会以非常积极的方式结束。 但我从来没有去过其他任何地方，有人当着我的面告诉我一件事（非常支持），然后在我不在的时候告诉其他人（在高级管理层）完全相反的事情。

前端近视

亚马逊的绝大多数软件工程师专门从事后端开发。 但该公司对前端开发的整体看法似乎陷入了 2015 年的泥潭。

作为亚马逊的常客，早在我在那里工作之前，我就注意到他们的用户体验很漂亮，嗯……缺乏。 现在我知道为什么了。

公平地说，他们通过网站成功地带来了数十亿美元的收入。 所以我不会说他们的前端界面不知何故“坏了”。 每当您运营一个规模如此庞大的网站时，肯定会有许多注意事项根本不适用于 99.99% 的其他网站。

我还要承认，Amazon 中有一些团队在使用现代标准进行前端工作。 但是看到有多少团队对现代前端功能视而不见有点令人不安。 哎呀，我什至目睹了对任何现代前端方法怀有敌意的团队。

在 Amazon 的内部知识库中，一些终生从事 Java 编码工作的人写了一篇长篇大论，谈论前端应用程序（和现代框架——如 React）是如何难以管理和不可扩展的。 这是一个相当长的阅读。 在我加入后不久，我问我们的一位 Java 开发人员为什么我们要以某种方式做事。 他的回应是……把那篇内部文章发给我。

没关系，有问题的文章是在 2017 年写的。 没关系，在文章本身，文章顶部早就有免责声明，声明其大部分论点现在都值得怀疑。 这些都不重要。 重要的是 JS 框架是“坏的”（mmmkay...）而老式的客户端-服务器架构是“好的”。

一线希望

我最后的观察带来了一线希望（至少对我而言）。 我们都知道，亚马逊只是过去六个月裁员数十人的众多大型科技公司之一。 虽然我知道裁员在宏观意义上是企业生活中的一个悲惨现实，但亚马逊竭尽全力确保他们围绕此事的内部和外部沟通绝对笨拙，坦率地说，不专业。 我不会在这里详细介绍所有这些。 我相信您已经阅读了科技/商业新闻中的文章。

上个月，Andy Jassy 带来了新的丑闻。 自 5 月 1 日起，他们要求每个人每周至少在办公室待三天。 我敢肯定，归根结底，还是会有一些人能够免于这个诏书的。 但迄今为止的每一个迹象都表明，几乎没有例外。

以一种令人作呕的方式，这实际上让我感到有点……如释重负。 你看，我住在佛罗里达。 我是一名远程工作者。 当我被录用时，我的 offer 明确表示我将成为一名远程工作者。 我的团队中还有其他人以同样的借口工作。 即使去年的那些烂事没有发生在我身上，即使我在一月份从未被解雇，我现在也会对整个情况感到压力和愤怒。

所以我想，归根结底，“地狱”的最后一年对我来说不会有任何好转，即使我在我的团队中从未遇到过任何问题，即使我从未在他们的任何裁员下被解雇. 听起来很奇怪，但这实际上让我对整个体验感觉“更好”了。

我不会试图粉饰这一点：亚马逊现在所做的是对他们在过去几年雇用的数十名员工的公然背叛，他们明确表示他们将远离他们。 这也是对许多西雅图员工的背叛，他们的生活发生了重大变化（比如……从西雅图搬到美国其他地区），因为他们一再得到保证，不会被迫回到办公室.

继续...

我已经在这个网站上发表了 112 篇文章（到目前为止）。 我写了所有其他的，希望能传播知识，向 Dev.to 社区学习，并促进讨论。 这个我在亚马逊的生活系列文章是我第一次在这里写任何不是为了这些目的的东西。 我为自己写了最后三篇文章。 正如我在第 1 部分中所述，这基本上是我的自我管理疗法——一种让我对过去一年经历的废话大喊大叫的方式。

话虽这么说，这个（不是那么少）故事并不是悲剧。 离得很远。 我目前正在评估潜在雇主提供的一份非常可靠的工作机会。 我希望在接下来的 coupla 日子里收到一些其他可靠的报价。

一个简单的事实是，不管我下个月在哪里工作，也不管这里的任何人如何看待我在亚马逊的故事——我都会没事的。 事实上，超越罚款。

正如您从这三篇文章中毫无疑问地推测的那样，我在亚马逊的体验中绝对有一些方面仍然让人感到糟糕透顶。 有一段时间，主要是从 2022 年 11 月到 2023 年 1 月，所有这一切都对我的身心健康造成了严重损害。

但你知道吗？ 那过去了。 生活仍在继续。 在你知道之前，我会惹恼一些新公司的人。

小心！

一旦暂停，bytebodger 将无法发表评论或发布帖子，直到他们的暂停被删除。

解除暂停后，bytebodger 将能够再次发表评论和发布帖子。

一旦取消发布，bytebodger 的所有帖子都将隐藏起来，只有他们自己可以访问。

如果 bytebodger 没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从他们的仪表板重新发布他们的帖子。

笔记：

感谢您维护 DEV 社区的安全。 您可以通过以下方式标记 bytebodger：

降低 bytebodger 的所有帖子的可见度

报告其他不当行为

取消标记 bytebodger 将恢复其帖子的默认可见性。



原文:

(You can read Part 1 of this article here: dev.to/bytebodger/what-it-was-like-to-c…. And Part 2 here: dev.to/bytebodger/what-it-was-like-to-c…)

If you've read any of the first two articles from this series, you already know that my Amazon experience was... suboptimal. But the first thing I have to acknowledge is that I don't actually know what it's like to code for Amazon in general. In fact, even if you've coded for Amazon in the past, or even if you're coding for them now, you don't know what it's like to code for them in general. The company's far too big and there are far too many devs strewn across myriad teams and locations for any single person to say, with authority, exactly what the overall dev experience is like.

In a company that large, all you can really do is assess your own experience. Some people may have had experiences similar to mine - or worse, even. But I'm also certain that some Amazon coders are perfectly happy there. It all depends, to some extent, on exactly which team/group you worked within, who exactly you worked for/with, and of course, what you brought to the experience.

But while I can't paint a broad, definitive picture of exactly what it's like for anyone, in any Amazon team/group, I did see enough to draw some curious conclusions.

Lip Service To "Principles"

Amazon constantly touts their "Leadership Principles". They ask you about them when you're interviewing. They talk about them in company meetings. On the surface, at least, they pour a lot of effort into these principles. (If you're curious, you can see them all here: www.amazon.jobs/content/en/our-workplac…)

Unfortunately, a lot of this emphasis is empty talk. (I know, I know. A megacorporation that doesn't always live up to its own stated ideals. SHOCKING!!!) I could go through them one-by-one and nitpick them based upon my short tenure there. And my perspective is, undoubtedly, biased by my own negative experiences and my own (admittedly brief) tenure. But in my experience, the most glaring example of a completely-failed "principle" is this:

Have Backbone; Disagree and Commit
Leaders are obligated to respectfully challenge decisions when they disagree, even when doing so is uncomfortable or exhausting. Leaders have conviction and are tenacious. They do not compromise for the sake of social cohesion. Once a decision is determined, they commit wholly.


I saw so many examples that made this principle an outright joke that I can't even list them all here. And I'm not just talking about my experience. I'm talking about things I witnessed in other areas of the organization, and things I was told directly by other employees.

I witnessed so many instances where people were simply afraid to speak up. I sat in meetings where no one but the "talking head" dared utter any kind of objection. And when they did bother to raise concerns, I also saw where those concerns were bulldozed because there was no alternative that pleased management.

A Telling Observation

When I wrote this article about working at Amazon (dev.to/bytebodger/what-its-like-to-code…) a senior dev from an entirely different part of the company reached out to me on Slack. He praised the article, thanked me for writing it, and said he was glad to see that I was getting along nicely.

I thanked him for his feedback. But I also told him that, in the time since I wrote the article, I'd run into some very difficult situations. (I didn't deluge him with the full, insanely-long story of everything that had happened. I just gave him the high-level bullet points.) His response was very telling.

He was Australian. He told me that, in his previous job, there were many times when the dev team would be discussing some particular bit of an application that had already been built. And frequently they would find themselves saying to each other, "Yeah... this is all a bit crap, isn't it?"

But he also told me that, when he came onboard at Amazon, he had very similar problems. Because, for a good bit after he was hired, no one wanted to hear any kinda dissenting opinions - even if they were only "dissenting" as a means to talk out the problem and to (hopefully) come to a better solution.

His specific advice was to lay low for a good while. Don't say much. Just kinda "go along" with whatever was being dictated to you. And then, only after you've been onboard for a good bit, maybe possibly speak up about a few targeted things.

To be clear, I get this. I'd be the first one to tell you that no one wants the Brand New Guy to show up, on Day 1, and immediately tell everyone that they've been doing crappy work. And that the legacy app is shite. And that all of their current practices should be thrown out the window.

But there's a difference between being an arrogant know-it-all versus just being... a humble - but confident - member of the team. Sometimes some of the best suggestions come from the new guys. Because everyone else has grown accustomed to everything as "the way it's always been done". As long as the New Guy isn't a cocky jerk, there's a lot of value in seeing how someone views the situation with a fresh set of eyes.

That's what Have Backbone; Disagree and Commit is supposed to be about. If you see something that seems "off"... say something. Talk about it with your colleagues. Or your manager. Or your stakeholders.

Sometimes, when you raise those concerns, you'll be "voted down". And that's fine. Other times, you may find that people actually agree with you - but there are other practical reasons why it will continue to be done that way. And again - that's fine. But it's far healthier to at least have those conversations, rather than being afraid that you'll be labeled as a malcontent merely because you raised reasonable concerns.

A Big Brother Mentality

Here's something that I encountered multiple times at Amazon. And I found it to be a bit... disturbing.

I'd be chatting with someone on Slack about something that seemed "off". Maybe it was about a project. Or about a particular person. Or even about the company in general. And in these scenarios, with multiple different people, the Slack conversation would end when the other person typed this:

Maybe we should delete this Slack conversation?


Now to be clear, we weren't talking about bombing the CEO's house. We weren't calling anyone an asshole. In fact, we weren't discussing anything in those Slack threads that I woulda been afraid to say to anyone else, directly to their face. We were just discussing problems that we'd run into, and potentially how to fix them.

And yet, on numerous occasions, my Slack counterpart would close the conversation by suggesting that we delete the thread. Think about that... Honestly, every time I encountered it, it felt rather chilling.

Blatant Backstabbing

After coding for a quarter century, I've lost track of the number of coworkers who simply didn't like me. Work at enough companies, with enough colleagues, and it's inevitable that some people simply won't "take" to you. But that's not what I'm talking about here.

Despite all the interpersonal conflicts I've navigated throughout my career, there is only one company where I've experienced open backstabbing. And of course, that company was Amazon.

When I say "backstabbing", I'm not referring to someone saying that I'm a jerk (take a number - it's a long line). Nor am I referring to someone badmouthing my code, or my deliverables, or anything about my work effort. I'm specifically talking about someone saying, to my face, that I'm doing great work. Then turning around, whenever a project suffers from any kinda setback, and telling everyone else that I've screwed everything up.

I've had people tell me, to my face, that they thought I really screwed something up. To be honest, once you get past the raw emotions of those moments, sometimes those interactions can end on an extremely positive note. But I've never been anywhere else where someone told me one (extremely supportive) thing to my face, and then told someone else (in senior management) the exact opposite when I wasn't there.

Frontend Myopia

The vast majority of Amazon's software engineers specialize in backend development. (Specifically, Java.) And there's nothing wrong with that. But the company's overall view on frontend development seems to be mired in 2015.

As a frequent Amazon customer, I'd noticed long before I ever worked there, that their UX was pretty, well... lacking. Now I know why.

To be fair, they successfully drive billions in revenue through their website. So I'm not gonna claim that their frontend interfaces are somehow "broken". And whenever you're operating a site that runs on that kinda epic scale, there are bound to be many considerations that simply don't apply to 99.99% of the other websites that are out there.

I'll also acknowledge that there are some teams in Amazon doing frontend work that utilizes modern standards. But it's kinda disturbing to see how many teams are oblivious to modern frontend capabilities. Heck, I even witnessed teams that were hostile toward any kinda modern frontend approaches.

In Amazon's internal knowledgebase, some lifelong Java coder wrote a long screed talking about how frontend apps (and modern frameworks - like React) were simply unmanageable and unscalable. It's quite a long read. Shortly after I came onboard, I was asking one of our Java devs why we were doing things a certain way. His response was... to send me that internal article.

Nevermind the fact that the article in question was written in 2017. And nevermind the fact that, on the article itself, there's long been a disclaimer, right at the top of the article, stating that most of its contentions are now in doubt. None of that mattered. All that mattered was that JS frameworks were "bad" (mmmkay...) and old-skool client-server architecture was "good".

A Silver Lining

My last observation comes with a bit of a silver lining (for me, at least). We all know that Amazon is just one of many Big Tech companies that's jettisoned scores of people in the last six months. While I understand that layoffs are, in a macro sense, a sad reality of corporate life, Amazon did everything they could to ensure that their internal and external communications around the matter were absolutely clumsy and, quite frankly, unprofessional. I'm not gonna go through all of that in detail here. I'm sure you've read the articles in the tech/business news.

Last month, a new bit of ugliness came from Andy Jassy. Effective May 1st, they're requiring everyone to be in the office at least three days per week. I'm sure that, in the final analysis, there will be some people who manage to get excused from this edict. But every indication so far is that there will be few exceptions.

In a sick sort of way, this actually made me feel kinda... relieved. You see, I live in Florida. I'm a remote worker. When I was hired, my offer specifically stated that I would be a remote worker. There were others on my team working under the same pretenses. And even if all the crap of the last year hadn't happened to me, and even if I was never laid off in January, I'd now be stressed and infuriated by the whole situation.

So I guess that, in the final analysis, the last year of "hell" wasn't gonna turn out any better for me, even if I never had any problems on my team and even if I was never booted under any of their layoffs. As odd as it sounds, that actually makes me feel somewhat "better" about the whole experience.

I'm not gonna make any attempt to sugarcoat this: What Amazon's doing now is an outright betrayal to the scores of people they hired over the last couple of years with the express understanding that they would be remote. It's also a betrayal to so many of their Seattle workers who made significant life changes (like... moving away from Seattle to other parts of the country) because they were given repeated assurances that they would not be forced to come back to the office.

Moving On...

I've put 112 articles on this site (so far). I wrote all of the others to, hopefully, spread knowledge, learn from the Dev.to community, and foster discussion. This little series of my-life-at-Amazon articles was the first time that I ever wrote anything here that was not for those purposes. I wrote these last three articles for me. As I stated in Part 1, this has basically been my self-administered therapy - a way for me to yell into the void about crap that I experienced over the last year.

That being said, this (not so little) tale is not a tragedy. Far from it. I'm currently evaluating one very-solid offer from a potential employer. I expect to receive a few other solid offers in the next coupla days.

The simple fact is that, regardless of where I'm working next month, and regardless of what anyone on here thinks about my Amazon tale - I'll be fine. Beyond fine, in fact.

As you've no-doubt surmised from these three articles, there are absolutely some aspects of my Amazon experience that still feel crappy as hell. And there was a period, mostly from November 2022 through January 2023, that all of this took a serious toll on my mental and physical health.

But you know what? That passes. Life goes on. And I'll be pissing people off at some new company before you know it.

Take care!

David Chibueze Ndubuisi - Mar 17

item.title: 构建可访问的 React.js 应用程序 | Building Accessible React.js Applications
item.description:
考纳哈桑

发表于 3 月 27 日

但是，重要的是要确保所有用户（包括残障人士）都可以访问使用 React 构建的应用程序。 在本文中，我们将通过涵盖一些技巧和最佳实践来提供有关如何构建易于访问的 React 应用程序的指南。

使用 ARIA 属性

例如，“aria-label”属性可用于为可见文本中尚未提供的按钮或链接提供标签。 这是一个代码示例：

 <input aria-describedby="email-description" type="email" /> <p id="email-description">Please enter your email address.</p>

实现键盘导航

键盘导航是辅助功能的一个重要方面，因为某些用户可能无法使用鼠标或触摸屏。 这是一个代码示例：

 <ul> <li tabIndex="0">Home</li> <li tabIndex="0">About</li> <li tabIndex="0">Contact</li> </ul>

为图像提供替代文本

替代文本（alt text）用于为无法看到图像的用户描述图像的内容。 这是一个代码示例：

 <img src="example.jpg" alt="A dog playing in a park" />

确保与辅助技术的兼容性

屏幕阅读器等辅助技术依赖于以与其软件兼容的方式构建 Web 内容。 提高可访问性的实用技巧

  • 使用语义 HTML 元素，例如“按钮”和“输入”，为辅助技术提供上下文。
  • 为表单元素使用描述性标签，为可能无法看到可视界面的用户提供上下文。
  • 使用高对比度的颜色和可读的字体，让有视觉障碍的用户更容易阅读文本。
  • 使用屏幕阅读器等辅助技术测试您的应用程序，以确保所有用户都可以访问它。

结论

通过遵循这些技巧和最佳实践，您可以构建可供所有用户（包括残障人士）使用的可访问 React 应用程序。 请记住使用 ARIA 属性，实现键盘导航，为图像提供替代文本，并确保与辅助技术的兼容性。 通过优先考虑可访问性，您可以为每个人创建一个更具包容性的网络。

一旦暂停，haszankauna 将无法发表评论或发布帖子，直到他们的暂停被解除。

解除暂停后，haszankauna 将能够再次发表评论和发布帖子。

一旦取消发布，haszankauna 的所有帖子都将隐藏起来，只有他们自己可以访问。

如果 haszankauna 没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从他们的仪表板重新发布他们的帖子。

笔记：

感谢您维护 DEV 社区的安全。 以下是标记 haszankauna 的方法：

降低 haszankauna 的所有帖子的可见度

报告其他不当行为

取消标记 haszankauna 将恢复其帖子的默认可见性。



原文:

Kauna Hassan

Posted on Mar 27

   

React.js is a popular JavaScript library used for building dynamic user interfaces. However, it is important to ensure that the applications built with React are accessible to all users, including those with disabilities. In this article, we will provide guidance on how to build accessible React applications by covering some tips and best practices.

Using ARIA Attributes

ARIA (Accessible Rich Internet Applications) attributes provide a way to make web content and applications more accessible to users with disabilities. React provides built-in support for ARIA attributes, which can be added to HTML elements using the "aria-" prefix. For example, the "aria-label" attribute can be used to provide a label for a button or link that is not already provided in the visible text. Here’s a code sample:

<input aria-describedby="email-description" type="email" />
<p id="email-description">Please enter your email address.</p>

Implementing Keyboard Navigation

Keyboard navigation is an important aspect of accessibility, as some users may not be able to use a mouse or touch screen. React provides support for keyboard navigation through the use of the "tabIndex" prop, which determines the order in which elements can be focused using the keyboard. Here’s a code sample:

<ul> <li tabIndex="0">Home</li>
 <li tabIndex="0">About</li>
 <li tabIndex="0">Contact</li>
</ul>

Providing Alternative Text for Images

Alternative text (alt text) is used to describe the content of an image for users who are unable to see it. React provides support for alt text through the use of the "alt" prop, which can be added to image elements. Here’s a code sample:

<img src="example.jpg" alt="A dog playing in a park" />

Ensuring Compatibility with Assistive Technologies

Assistive technologies, such as screen readers, rely on web content to be structured in a way that is compatible with their software. React provides support for accessibility by generating semantic HTML, which is compatible with assistive technologies.

Practical Tips for Improving Accessibility

  • Use semantic HTML elements, such as "button" and "input", to provide context to assistive technologies.
  • Use descriptive labels for form elements to provide context to users who may not be able to see the visual interface.
  • Use high-contrast colors and readable fonts to make text easier to read for users with visual impairments.
  • Test your application with assistive technologies, such as screen readers, to ensure that it is accessible to all users.

Conclusion

By following these tips and best practices, you can build accessible React applications that are usable by all users, including those with disabilities. Remember to use ARIA attributes, implement keyboard navigation, provide alternative text for images, and ensure compatibility with assistive technologies. By making accessibility a priority, you can create a more inclusive web for everyone.

Rishikesh Vedpathak - Mar 12

Ram Maheshwari ⚡ - Mar 23

item.title: 不要害怕 AI 的兴起：认识 AltQ，您的新编码伙伴，并解锁无尽的机会！ | Fear Not the Rise of AI: Meet AltQ, Your New Coding Companion, and Unlock Endless Opportunities!
item.description:
引入 AltQ，这个非常酷的代码助手，它会让你怀疑你是否还需要咖啡来编写代码。
它是一个 Visual Code 扩展，可与您的编码环境无缝集成。

想象一下：你告诉 AltQ 你需要什么，然后
噗——它像没什么大不了的一样吐出代码！

准备好被惊艳到吧，因为 AltQ 和我刚刚联手打造了一款出色的小型坦克游戏，其中包含用户控件、敌人 AI、游戏状态和简约的界面！

但是，等等，还有更多：我们不仅仅停留在构建游戏元素上，我们还开发了一个简单的游戏引擎来实现我们的坦克式愿景。

这就像有一个编码助手，他确切地知道我想要什么，预测我的需求并帮助我冲破障碍，就像瞄准坦克射击一样。

在 AltQ 的指导和支持下，我们轻而易举地完成了开发过程，证明了在编码方面，两个脑袋（或一个人和一个 AI）绝对比一个脑袋好！

它是关于创建一个真正了解您的项目和目标的代码助手。

关键区别：AltQ 深入了解您的工作背景，掌握项目的本质并轻松解决日常问题。

渴望体验 AltQ 的强大之处？ 这是为 Visual Code 安装和配置它的便捷指南。 这就像与您的新 AI 好友安排游戏约会一样！

第 1 步：前往 Visual Code Marketplace 并搜索 AltQ 扩展。 找到它后，单击“安装”按钮将其添加到您的编码库中。

第 2 步：要让奇迹发生，您需要一个来自 OpenAI 的 API 密钥。 访问 OpenAI 网站，注册或登录，然后拿到那把珍贵的钥匙。

第 3 步：有了您的 API 密钥，就可以配置 AltQ 了。 您可以通过以下两种方式之一执行此操作：

  • 选项 A：深入 Visual Code 的设置，找到 AltQ 扩展设置，然后输入您的 API 密钥。
  • 选项 B：如果您更喜欢命令提示符类型的编码器，您也可以在第一次运行 AltQ 提示符命令时输入您的 API 密钥。

就是这样！ 只需按住Alt key + Q ，您就可以开始使用您值得信赖的 AI 助手 AltQ 进行编码冒险了。 编程愉快！

打不过AI？ 加入他们，带领他们，继续摇滚！

解决房间里的大象问题：担心因 AI（如 AltQ）窃取编码风头而导致失业。

无需恐慌！ 重点从手动编码转移到战略思考和解决问题——是时候升级了。

拥抱变化，磨练新技能，成为 AI 驱动的编程世界的开拓者。

突出 AltQ 的杀手级功能之一：将内存密集型任务外包给 AI，这样您就可以专注于创造力。 人类的记忆是有限度的，但有了像 AltQ 这样的代码助手，您可以打破这些界限，在编程中达到新的高度。 突出 AltQ 的杀手级功能之一：将内存密集型任务外包给 AI，这样您就可以专注于创造力。 人类的记忆是有限度的，但有了像 AltQ 这样的代码助手，您可以打破这些界限，在编程中达到新的高度。

让 AI 处理细节并记住无数实体，同时您将 A-game 带到桌面并以前所未有的方式进行创新。

改变游戏规则的 AI 助手，是时候加入编码革命了！

在您一头扎进 AltQ 的世界之前，让我们花点时间记住它仍处于早期的 alpha 阶段。 就像刚出生的小猫找到立足点一样，AltQ 需要成长。

目前，AltQ 依赖 GPT-3 模型来生成答案。 虽然它非常令人印象深刻，但它有点过时并且可能需要一些耐心和坚持才能达到预期的结果。

但不要害怕，编码爱好者们！ 我制定了改进引擎和改进整个过程的宏伟计划——这只是激动人心的旅程的第一步。

所以，系好安全带，加入我，帮助 AltQ 从 alpha 版本发展成为改变游戏规则的代码助手！

等待 GPT-4：AltQ 的美好未来和我们的共同努力

虽然当前的 GPT-3.5 API 并不完全适合 AltQ，但未来一片光明！ 我有机会修补 GPT-4，让我告诉你——它简直令人难以置信。

不幸的是，我的 GPT-4 API 访问仍在候补名单上。
让我们发出一些声音！ 我们可以一起提高对 AltQ 的认识，并通过一些宣传，我们可能会尽快获得 API 访问权限。

因此，广为宣传，分享您的兴奋之情，让我们借助 GPT-4 的力量帮助 AltQ 充分发挥其潜力。 想象一下：AltQ 成为您的得力助手，充分了解您的项目，了解每一行代码的来龙去脉，并掌握每个变量的影响。

凭借其令人难以置信的 AI 功能，AltQ 将代表您创建所需的功能和项目工件，让您的编码生活变得更加轻松。

但它并不止于此——AltQ 将释放您的思维带宽，让您专注于应对真正复杂的挑战并做出突破性的发现。

让我们一起将这一愿景变为现实，并以 AltQ 作为我们可信赖的伙伴来塑造 AI 驱动的代码协助的未来——不是抢走工作的“天网”，而是对所有开发人员都有帮助的伙伴。

原文:

   

Introducing AltQ, the code assistant so cool, it'll make you question if you even need coffee to code anymore.
It's a Visual Code extension that seamlessly integrates with your coding environment.

Picture this: You tell AltQ what you need, and
poof – it spits out the code like it's no big deal!

AltQ is like a programmer's dream come true – it understands human lingo and turns it into code.

AltQ ensures a smooth coding experience, making it feel like you have your own dedicated protégé ready to execute any task you assign.

AltQ in Action: Creating a Tank Game Together Like a Coding Dream Team

Get ready to be amazed, because AltQ and I just joined forces to create a fantastic little tank game, complete with user controls, enemy AI, game states, and a minimalistic interface!

But wait, there's more: we didn't just stop at building the game elements, we also whipped up a simple game engine to bring our tank-tastic vision to life.

It was like having a coding sidekick who knew exactly what I wanted, anticipating my needs and helping me blast through obstacles like a well-aimed tank shot.

With AltQ's guidance and support, we breezed through the development process, proving that when it comes to coding, two heads (or one human and one AI) are definitely better than one!

AltQ vs. Copilot and Rest: A Unique Approach to Code Assistance.

Other code assistants like Copilot and ChatGPT-integrated Visual Code have their charm, but AltQ brings a unique flavor to the coding fiesta.

AltQ isn't just about offering snippets or chatting; it's about creating a code assistant that truly understands your project and your goals.

The key differentiator: AltQ dives deep into the context of your work, grasping the essence of your project and tackling routine problems with ease.

Ready, Set, AltQ: A Quick Guide to Installation and Setup

Eager to experience the awesomeness of AltQ? Here's your handy-dandy guide to getting it installed and configured for Visual Code. It's like setting up a playdate with your new AI buddy!

Step 1: Head over to the Visual Code Marketplace and search for the AltQ extension. Once you find it, click the "Install" button to add it to your coding arsenal.

Step 2: To make the magic happen, you'll need an API key from OpenAI. Visit the OpenAI website, sign up or log in, and get your hands on that precious key.

Step 3: With your API key in hand, it's time to configure AltQ. You can do this in one of two ways:

  • Option A: Dive into the settings of Visual Code, locate the AltQ extension settings, and enter your API key.
  • Option B: If you're more of a command prompt kind of coder, you can also enter your API key the first time you run the AltQ prompt command.

And that's it! Simply hold the Alt key + Q, and you're all set to start your coding adventure with AltQ, your trusty AI sidekick. Happy programming!

Can't Beat AI? Join 'em, Lead 'em, and Rock On!

Addressing the elephant in the room: the fear of job loss due to AI (like AltQ) stealing the coding thunder.

No need to panic! The focus shifts from manual coding to strategic thinking and problem-solving – it's time to level up.

Embrace the change, hone new skills, and become a trailblazer in the AI-powered world of programming.

Highlighting one of AltQ's killer features: outsourcing memory-intensive tasks to AI, so you can focus on being creative. Human memory has limits, but with code assistants like AltQ, you can break those boundaries and reach new heights in programming. Highlighting one of AltQ's killer features: outsourcing memory-intensive tasks to AI, so you can focus on being creative. Human memory has limits, but with code assistants like AltQ, you can break those boundaries and reach new heights in programming.

Let AI handle the details and remember countless entities, while you bring your A-game to the table and innovate like never before.

AI assistants changing the game, and it's time to jump on board the coding revolution!

AltQ Alpha: A Work in Progress with Big Dreams and Plans

Before you dive headfirst into the world of AltQ, let's take a moment to remember that it's still in its early alpha stage. Like a newborn kitten finding its footing, AltQ has some growing to do.

Currently, AltQ relies on the GPT-3 model to generate answers. While it's quite impressive, it's a bit outdated and might require some patience and persistence to achieve the desired results.

But fear not, fellow coding enthusiasts! I've got grand plans to refine the engine and improve the entire process – this is just the first step on the exciting journey.

So, buckle up and join me as we help AltQ evolve from an alpha version into the game-changing code assistant!

Awaiting GPT-4: AltQ's Bright Future and Our Collective Efforts

While the current GPT-3.5 API isn't quite the perfect fit for AltQ, the future is looking bright! I've had the chance to tinker with GPT-4, and let me tell you – it's downright incredible.

Unfortunately, my GPT-4 API access is still on the waitlist.
Let's make some noise! Together, we can raise awareness about AltQ, and with a little buzz, we might just get that API access sooner rather than later.

So, spread the word, share your excitement, and let's help AltQ reach its full potential with the power of GPT-4. The future of AI-driven code assistance awaits!

The Future of AltQ: A Code Assistant That Knows Your Project Inside Out

Picture this: AltQ becomes your go-to companion, fully understanding your project, knowing the ins and outs of each line of code, and grasping the impact of every variable.

With its incredible AI capabilities, AltQ will create required features and project artifacts on your behalf, making your coding life a whole lot easier.

But it doesn't stop there – AltQ will free up your mental bandwidth, allowing you to focus on tackling the truly complex challenges and making groundbreaking discoveries.

Together, let's turn this vision into reality and shape the future of AI-driven code assistance with AltQ as our trusty sidekick – not a job-stealing "Skynet", but a helpful companion for all developers.

AltQ

Pragyan Tripathi - Feb 17

Ekaterine Mitagvaria - Mar 15

item.title: 我的 LinkedIn 课程“学习 GitHub”的数字 | My LinkedIn Course "Learning GitHub" by numbers
item.description:
几个月前，我开始谈论我作为 LinkedIn Learning 技术讲师的经历。

到 1 月底，我的课程“学习 GitHub”已经发布，人们开始观看它。

在这篇文章中，我将不时发布一些更新，以查看课程的进展情况，并特别了解用户反馈以及我是否可以改进任何内容。

接近 3 月底时，我的 LinkedIn 课程吸引了 3000 名学习者。 这是我为自己设定的目标之一：1k、3k、5k、10k、20k。 这些几乎就是我摆在我面前的目标。 这意味着在过去的 60 天里（自课程发布以来）我每天接触到大约 55 名学习者。 实际上人数有所增加，因为一个月后我“只有”1,000 名学员。

很可能课程链接现在比以前更明显。

为了跟踪课程随时间的演变，我为自己创建了一个 excel 文件，其中包含一些图表和一些我不时更新的统计数据，几乎每两天更新一次，而且总是在午餐后更新。
一些有趣的事情已经出来了。

该课程有3304 名学员观看了至少 20% 的课程。
这意味着如果相同百分比的人写了评论

  • 平均评分为： 4.6 。 唯一非常糟糕的评论是关于我的英语口音，但这是我知道的，我正在努力。

    顺便说一句，这个差评来自美国人。 这意味着这对我们来说还不够，但也许对于非母语人士来说，我的英语还可以。

    关于课程的附加信息

    老实说，我对两个月后的结果很满意。
    期待4月份的数据！

    您是否有兴趣学习 GitHub 但不知道从哪里开始？ 试试我在 LinkedIn Learning 上的课程： Learning GitHub 。

    感谢您阅读这篇文章，希望您觉得它有趣！

    随时关注我，以便在新文章发布时收到通知🙂

    莱昂纳多蒙蒂尼 - 1 月 10 日

    德米特里·多罗戈伊 - 22 年 12 月 19 日

    一旦被暂停，“这就是学习”将无法发表评论或发布帖子，直到他们的暂停被取消。

    一旦解除暂停，这就是学习将能够再次发表评论和发布帖子。

    一旦取消发布，this-is-learning 的所有帖子都将隐藏起来，只有他们自己可以访问。

    如果“这就是学习”没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从他们的仪表板重新发布他们的帖子。

    笔记：

    感谢您维护 DEV 社区的安全。 您可以通过以下方式标记“正在学习”：

    降低 this-is-learning 的所有帖子的可见度

    报告其他不当行为

取消标记“这就是学习”将恢复其帖子的默认可见性。



原文:

A few months ago I started to talk about my journey as a Technical Instructor for LinkedIn Learning.

By the end of January my course "Learning GitHub" has been released and people start to watch it.

In this post I will publish some updates time to time to see how the course is going and and understand especially user feedback and whether I can improve anything.

March 2023

Very near the end of March I reached 3k learners on my LinkedIn course. It was one of the goals I set for myself: 1k, 3k, 5k, 10k, 20k. Those are pretty much the goals I put in front of me. This means that I reached about 55 learners each day for the previous 60 days (since the course was released). Actually there was an increase in numbers because after one month I had "only" 1,000 learners.

Most likely the course link is now much more visible than before.

To keep track of the evolution of the course over time, I created myself an excel file with some graphs and some statistics that I go to update from time to time, almost once every two days and always after lunch.
It seems that LinkedIn updates the statistics after lunch.

Some interesting things have come out.

The course reached 3304 learners with at least 20% of the course watched.
92 people shared the course on LinkedIn and **86 **liked the course.

111 learners reviewed my course and based on the percentage:

  • 78 people reviewed with 5 ⭐
  • 22 people reviewed with 4 ⭐
  • 4 people reviewed with 3 ⭐
  • 3 people reviewed with 2 ⭐
  • 1 people reviewed with 1 ⭐

This means if the same percentage of people wrote a review

  • 2313 people reviewed with 5 ⭐
  • 661 people reviewed with 4 ⭐
  • 132 people reviewed with 3 ⭐
  • 99 people reviewed with 2 ⭐
  • 17 people reviewed with 1 ⭐

The average rating is: 4.6. The only very bad reviews are about my English accent, but it's something I know and I am working on it.

By the way, this bad reviews came from American people. It means it's not enough for us but maybe for a non-native my English is ok.

Additional info about the course

Honestly I am happy about the result after two months.
Looking forward for the April statistics!

Are you interested in learning GitHub but don't know where to start? Try my course on LinkedIn Learning: Learning GitHub.

Thanks for reading this post, I hope you found it interesting!

Feel free to follow me to get notified when new articles are out 🙂

Leonardo Montini - Jan 10

Pachi 🪐 (she/her/ela) - Jan 9

Dmitry Dorogoy - Dec 19 '22
